original_abstract:
We report the development of a new family of ruthenium complexes, defined by the formula [Ru(n)(tda)(py)2](m+) (where tda²⁻ is [2,2':6',2″-terpyridine]-6,6″-dicarboxylate). Focused characterization reveals that the species [Ru(IV)(OH)(tda-κ-N3O)(py)2]⁺, designated as 4H⁺, serves as an exceptionally potent catalyst for water oxidation. This catalyst is generated through the hydroxo coordination of its precursor, 3²⁺, under basic conditions. We employed a multidimensional approach to characterize these complexes, utilizing analytical, spectroscopic (UV-vis, NMR, EPR), and electrochemical methods (CV, DPV, coulometry), supplemented by solid-state X-ray diffraction and computational modeling.

Structural analysis highlights a dynamic coordination environment: in its Ru(IV) state, the metal center adopts a seven-coordinate, diamagnetic geometry. Conversely, in the Ru(II) state, the complex shifts to a six-coordinate diamagnetic structure featuring an unbonded, "dangling" carboxylate group. The Ru(III) state represents a structural midpoint between these two configurations. While the precursor 3²⁺ remains catalytically inactive, the 4H⁺ derivative exhibits record-breaking performance. Foot-of-the-wave analysis reveals extraordinary turnover frequencies (TOF) of 8,000 s⁻¹ at pH 7.0 and up to 50,000 s⁻¹ at pH 10.0. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations elucidate the full catalytic cycle, identifying the dangling carboxylate as a critical functional element that lowers the activation energy required for O–O bond formation.
humanized_abstract:
Here we report the synthesis and characterization of a new family of ruthenium complexes given by the formula [Ru(n)(tda)(py)2](m+); where tda²⁻ is the ligand [2,2':6',2″-terpyridine]-6,6″-dicarboxylate. Detailed analysis of these complexes reveals that [Ru(IV)(OH)(tda-κ-N3O)(py)2]⁺; named 4H⁺ is an extremely efficient catalyst for the water oxidation reaction. It is produced in situ by hydroxo coordination to the precursor 3²⁺ at basic pH and the complex has been characterized with a suite of analytical, spectroscopic (UV-vis, NMR, EPR) and electrochemical (CV, DPV, coulometry) methods as well as by solid-state X-ray diffraction and density functional theory calculations.

This study characterized the structure of this elusive redox active complex, and showed that it changes coordination environment during catalysis: the Ru(IV) species is 7-coordinate and diamagnetic, while the Ru(II) species is 6-coordinate and also diamagnetic with an uncoordinated carboxylate as a "dangling" arm, and that the Ru(III) species is intermediate in structure. The precursor 3²⁺ species shows little catalytic activity, while the 4H⁺ form of this species has the highest known TOF, 8000 s⁻¹ at pH 7 and 50,000 s⁻¹ at pH 10. Using a detailed DFT study on the entire catalytic cycle showed that the dangling carboxylate has an important role by lowering the barrier of the O–O bond formation.

document.output:
Here we report the synthesis and characterization of a new family of ruthenium complexes given by the formula [Ru(n)(tda)(py)2](m+); where tda²⁻ is the ligand [2,2':6',2″-terpyridine]-6,6″-dicarboxylate. This study characterized the structure of this elusive redox active complex, and showed that it changes coordination environment during catalysis: the Ru(IV) species is 7-coordinate and diamagnetic, while the Ru(II) species is 6-coordinate and also diamagnetic with an uncoordinated carboxylate as a "dangling" arm, and that the Ru(III) species is intermediate in structure. document.input:
We report the development of a new family of ruthenium complexes, defined by the formula [Ru(n)(tda)(py)2](m+) (where tda²⁻ is [2,2':6',2″-terpyridine]-6,6″-dicarboxylate).
